definition: To *distill* is to heat a liquid until it vaporizes, then cool the vapor so it condenses and drips back pure into a receiver, leaving the dross behind. It is separation by rising and falling — distinct from *sublimation*, in which a solid passes straight to vapor without melting. In the alchemist's alembic and retort, distillation drew off spirits, essential oils, and the burning *aqua ardens* of wine.
history: Latin *destillare*, 'to drip down' (*de-* plus *stillare*, 'to drip,' from *stilla*, 'a drop'), names the process by its humblest sign: the steady fall of purified drops from the still's beak. Greek alchemy built the apparatus early — the *ambix*, through Arabic *al-anbiq*, gives us 'alembic.' The turn is figurative and durable: to distill became to reduce anything to its essence. Kieckhefer calls a medieval leechbook a reasonably sober distillation of classical medicine, and Renaissance writers say Agrippa skilfully distilled his magic from Ficino and Pico. Magic: The Gathering keeps both the vessel's steam and the metaphor's purity.

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
def_sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

def_sources.label: Greco-Egyptian alchemy (on the ambix / alembic)
def_sources.abstract: Traces how alchemy first took shape in ancient Alexandria, Egypt, where Greek philosophical ideas blended with hands-on Egyptian craft knowledge like metalworking and dyeing, forming the roots of what would eventually become chemistry.

etymology.origin: Latin destillare 'to drip down' (de- + stillare 'to drip', stilla 'a drop')
etymology.note: The whole apparatus is named for dripping; 'still' (the device) is just a clipping of 'distill'.

flavor_snippets.html: "Breath of basilisk, sting of scorpion, or medusa's tears? All may be <mark>distilled</mark>, for a price."
The assassins of the Ochran <mark>distill</mark> toxins from the remains of their previous victims.
Esika alone knows how to <mark>distill</mark> the essence of the World Tree into the Cosmos Elixir, a magical substance which grants the Skoti god-family immortality.

"I think it no accident that every civilized people has discovered the art of <mark>distillation</mark>."
—Rofellos of Llanowar
